data.title: Why Onto Innovation Is A Still A Buy After More Than Doubling In 4 Years
data.description:
In 2022, Onto Innovation (ONTO) offered investors an entry point after the stock dipped. In that time, the company rewarded shareholders with an over 220% return. This beat the S&P 500 (IVV), which gained 59.45%.

On April 17, Stifel Financial upgraded ONTO stock to a buy. It set a $350 price target. Will Onto meet Stifel’s price target this year? Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating also raised its rating to a ‘Strong Buy’ on April 17, 2026. Instead of relying on one analyst’s opinion that sent the stock up by 8.8% on the day, investors should assess Onto’s recent fundamental changes.

Mixed Fiscal Fourth Quarter Results

In the fourth quarter, Onto posted a non-GAAP earnings per share of $1.26, meeting expectations. Revenue grew by 1.1% Y/Y to $267 million, barely missing expectations. EPS GAAP actual also missed the analyst forecast. ONTO stock rose from the $200 trading range at the time to close at $290.76 on April 17.

Onto posted record revenue in Q4 and the full year. It acquired several product lines from Semilab International. It shipped several next-generation Dragonfly systems for the high-resolution, 2D applications market. The product achieved a successful qualification on April 16. Onto expects initial shipments in June.

The qualification process completes the process for the Dragonfly G5 system. Features include new proprietary optics, illumination, and algorithms that enhance visibility and throughput. This appeals to customers, since it lowers the overall cost of ownership. Onto cited industry analyst viewpoints on the demand for AI devices. Enabling them would advance packaging, a market that will grow by 30% yearly in the next two years.

Markets rewarded investors who invested in process control. Aehr Systems (AEHR), which provides test and burn-in solutions for the silicon carbide (“SiC”) market, rose eightfold in the last year.

Onto said in its Q4 and full-year press release that “With global AI investment fueling a robust upcycle in semiconductor capital equipment spending, our deep and broad portfolio across advanced packaging, advanced nodes, and specialty devices positions us to capitalize on the high-growth segments of the market.”

Onto is a downstream supplier to the growing AI market. After markets sent NVIDIA (NVDA) to a $4.9 trillion market capitalization, firms “downstream” to AI are rising. I provided coverage for firms benefiting from investments from NVIDIA. They included Nokia (NOK), Ciena (CIEN), Coherent (COHR), and Lumentum (LITE). More recently covered, Ericsson (ERIC), which is not a beneficiary of NVIDIA’s investment, cited its low-cost 5G infrastructure as appealing for AI markets.

Minimal Revenue Growth

Onto citing AI as a growth catalyst is at odds with its 1.1% Y/Y revenue increase. Gross margin also fell to 46.4%, compared to 50.2% in Q4/2024. Non-GAAP gross margin was 54.6%, compared to 54.5% the year before.

Advanced packaging and specialty devices accounted for more than half of Onto’s revenue in the last quarter. Revenue from advanced nodes more than doubled in the full year, lifted by growth in both DRAM and logic (75% of total). Cash generation of $95 million was a record quarter for Onto. The firm applied the One Big Beautiful Bill Tax Act to accelerate the expensing of certain research and development costs.

Stock markets are a forward-pricing machine, so it is assigning a valuation based on Onto’s packaging growth of 30%. Onto CEO Michael Plisinski said that Wafer Fabrication Equipment (“WFE”) sales are hard to track. It recently added advanced packaging orders. Additionally, it needed to include construction costs. Large device manufacturers and outsourcing semiconductor assembly and test firms, along with smaller players in the market, will consider providing alternative solutions. Despite the uncertainties, investors are pricing in an optimistic outlook.

Onto applied its historical capacity to prepare for a $2 billion run rate. By adding extended factories in the next few quarters, its capacity will increase.

Onto Stock Grades

Onto’s quant history rose from a sell/strong sell up until November 2025. Afterward, its rating cycled between a hold and buy/strong buy.

As shown above, Onto stock has held a Buy rating since last month.

In 2026, Onto is the second-best-performing stock. Teradyne (TER) outperformed it, while Lam Research (LRCX), KLA (KLAC), Nova (NVMI), and Applied Materials (AMAT) trailed its performance:

It ranks 15th in the sector and third place in the industry rank. It scores an A on the stock momentum and EPS revisions grades, as do its peers. However, its valuation of “D” is relatively the best.

Investors get a company with strong growth and profitability (graded B and B+, respectively).

Your Takeaway

Markets rewarded momentum traders who bought shares of Onto Innovation. Investors who are seeking value might consider this stock, too. It offers relative value compared to its peers. Just as the breakout in Aehr Test Systems and Lumentum showed no signs of reversing, the same appears true for Onto. Until that uptrend breaks down, Onto earns a reiterated buy rating four years after I initiated coverage on the company.

data.title: Week Ahead: Dollar And Stocks Due For Corrections?
data.description:
Progress in US-Iran negotiations and a ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon fed investor optimism. The US S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq reached new record highs, with multi-week rallies in tow. June WTI tumbled 7.6% last week, after the 8.6% drop the previous week. It briefly traded below $79 a barrel, down from the peak on March 9 (~$104.35). Interest rate expectations also shifted. In Europe, the two-year yields fell by 20-25 bp last week. The US two-year yield fell by a more modest 7-8 bp. Although Japan's two-year yield slipped almost three basis points, there was a more dramatic move in the swaps market, which dramatically adjusted the odds of a rate hike by the Bank of Japan later this month to less than 20% from around 55% a week ago.

Developments in the Middle East may be more important than the high-frequency economic data. The risk is that the market is getting ahead of itself and the situation is likely to be clarified over the weekend. US earnings season features tech and airlines next week. The dollar fell against all the G10 currencies last week but recovered from its worst levels in the waning hours of last week's activity. After rallying last month, the dollar has unwound a good part of those gains. Like the 13-day rally in the Nasdaq is an extreme, the Dollar Index (DXY) has fallen for nine of the past 10 sessions. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation of changes in the Dollar Index and NASDAQ is a little more than -0.60, which is the most since the end of 2023.

US

Drivers: The prospect that the Middle East war can end boosted risk appetites and weighed on the US dollar. The Dollar Index fell for the third consecutive week, matching its longest losing streak in a year. The Fed funds futures market is discounting an almost 65% chance of a cut before the end of the year, the highest probability in a month.

Data: Stronger auto sales and a dramatic rise in gasoline prices likely lifted March retail sales. Excluding autos and gasoline, retail sales may have edged up by 0.1-0.2%. Outside of retail sales, the other US high-frequency data are surveys. April Fed surveys (Philadelphia non-manufacturing survey and KC Fed's manufacturing and non-manufacturing) and the preliminary April PMI will likely pick up some of the disruption from the Middle East war. Q4 25 GDP was reported initially at 1.4% (annualized). It was halved in the first revision and to 0.5% in the second revision. The Atlanta Fed nowcast is for Q1 26 at 1.3%. It will be updated after the retail sales and business inventories on Tuesday. Also, Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing is scheduled for next week but could be postponed. As long as the investigations into the Fed continue, and the president indicated they would, there appears to be a blocking majority on the Senate Banking Committee. Our understanding is that if a successor has not been confirmed by the end of Powell's term as chair (May 15), he would likely be picked by the board to continue in that post. His term as governor does not end until 2028, and we suspect the odds favor him continuing to serve to protect what appears to be a strong conviction of the importance of the Federal Reserve's independence.

Prices: The Dollar Index fell for eight consecutive sessions through April 15. That matched its longest losing streak in 15 years. It fell through 97.65, its lowest level since the war began, and met the (50%) retracement of the rally from the year's low recorded in late January around 95.55. The Dollar Index recovered through most of the North American session before the weekend and reached almost 98.25. The momentum indicators are overextended but do not prevent additional near-term losses. Still, a move above 98.40-50 would suggest a near-term bottom is in place.

EMU

Drivers: The euro benefitted from the market's stronger appetite for risk and the broad dollar pullback more than a particular European development. At its peak on March 24, the swaps market discounted three rate hikes fully by the ECB this year and about a 25% chance of a fourth. It now has two hikes discounted and less than a 10% chance of a third. This still seems excessive. At its most extreme last month, the swaps market had an 85% chance of an April hike. It has been scaled back to about 12%.

Data: The preliminary April PMI is the highlight. Recall that the composite (output) had begun faltering. It rose from last June through November. It has now slowed three of the four months through March. The general pattern holds for both services and manufacturing. The Middle East war is disruptive on many channels, and it would be surprising if the March data fully reflected the impact. The same is true of Germany's ZEW and IFO April surveys, also due.

Prices: The euro has risen in nine of the past 10 sessions. Ahead of the weekend, it took out the high set before the war began (slightly below $1.1830) and proceeded to sell off around $1.1760. The potential key reversal warns that despite the momentum indicators still rising, the euro's four-cent rally over the past month may be over. If so, the first corrective target may be in the $1.1675-$1.1700 area.

PRC

Drivers: The PBOC has continued its campaign that began last May or June to appreciate the yuan. It has consistently been lowering the dollar's reference rate and now is at a three-year low. The campaign does not appear over even though the end point is not clear. Judging by several bank recommendations, portfolio flows appear supportive.

Data: Chinese banks will set their loan prime rates on April 20. The one-year rate is at 3.0% and the five-year rate is at 3.50%. Before the Middle East war, expecting more monetary easing seemed reasonable, but now it looks less certain. In any event, a clearer signal from the PBOC may be necessary for the loan prime rates to be changed. The increased use of China's International Payments System (CIPS) to settle and clear international transactions would seem to make less relevant, when taken in isolation, the use of the yuan on the SWIFT global payment system. On SWIFT, the yuan's share of payments peaked in July 2024 near 4.75%. In February, its share was about 2.75%.

Prices: The PBOC has continued to allow gradual appreciation of the yuan. In the offshore market, it rose to its best level since February 2023. The market has been encouraged by the central bank's guidance through the setting of the daily reference rate. It was set at its highest level against the dollar since April 2023 last week. The greenback found support near CNH6.80. The CNH6.85-CNH6.86 area may offer a nearby cap.

Japan

Drivers: There are three significant influences on the dollar-yen. The first is the general direction of the dollar. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index is at 0.75. The second is the change in the 10-year US yield. The correlation with the exchange rate is near 0.65, the highest since last October. Third, there is a risk element to the yen as well. The rolling 30-day correlation of change in the exchange rate and the S&P 500 is around -0.55, the most extreme since late 2022.

Data: While Japan has a busy calendar of high-frequency reports, they will likely have minimal impact on expectations for the BOJ meeting. The national March CPI, for example, was anticipated by the Tokyo figures a few weeks ago. Tokyo's year-over-year headline and core CPI measures edged 0.1% lower. The BOJ targets the core CPI rate, and in February, it stood at 1.6%. It was the first time since March 2022 that it was below the 2% target. One of the few things that most international economists might be able to agree on is that the Japanese yen is undervalued. And yet, it has not recorded an annual trade surplus since 2020. The deficit is shrinking. In the first two months of the year, Japan's trade deficit was around half the size of the shortfall in Jan-Feb 2025. Yet it seems like the terms of trade (export/import price indices) are going against it. The preliminary March PMI is due, but the market does not typically respond to it. The February tertiary industry index will help economists forecast GDP, but growth this quarter seems on par with Q4 25's 1.3% annualized. Meanwhile, BOJ Governor Ueda's comments were not strong enough to arrest the decline in expectation of a rate cut at the end of the month. In the past two weeks, the odds of a hike in the swaps market went from around 67% to less than 17%. We had thought it was more likely but recognize that the Bank of Japan will most likely not surprise the market.

Prices: After the dollar was turned back from its approach of JPY160 at the start of last week, it reached JPY157.60 at the end of the week. Ahead of the weekend, the greenback posted an outside down day by trading on both sides of the previous day's range, but it did not settle below its low (~JPY158.25), neutralizing the technical signal. The momentum indicators are falling, and the dollar looks soft. The fact that the dollar fell for the third consecutive week against the yen underscores our argument that despite the finance minister’s comments about being willing to take "bold action," the conditions for material intervention are not present.

UK

Drivers: Sterling benefits from a weaker dollar. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation between changes in sterling and the Dollar Index is almost -0.90, the most in six months. Sterling's correlation with changes in the US two-year yield is -0.33. The inversion is intuitively understandable, higher US rates, weaker sterling. What may be surprising is that the correlation between sterling and changes in the two-year UK yield is also inverse, though slightly (-0.12). Over the last two years, the rolling 30-day correlation has spent more time inverted than positive.

Data: It is an important week for UK data. While data feeds into the central bank's assessment, the bar for a change in policy next week seems high. Updates on the labor market, retail sales, and inflation are due. The UK is experiencing one of the strongest CPI increases and is one of the weakest major economies. The preliminary April PMI is due as well. In March, the composite dropped to its lowest level, 50.3, since last April, when it dipped below the 50 boom/bust level in a bit of a statistical quirk. It jumped back the following month. The data are unlikely to change anyone's mind that the BOE will stand pat even though there may be a dissent in favor of a hike. Recall that the March decision to leave policy unchanged was the first unanimous decision in 4.5 years.

Prices: Sterling stalled around $1.36 last week. Some frustrated late longs look to have bailed and sent sterling back to slightly below $1.3515 in late dealings before the weekend. By settling 1/100 of a cent below the previous day's range, sterling posted a potentially key reversal. It recorded a four-month low at the end of March near $1.3160, and with last week's advance, it met the (61.8%) retracement of the losses from nearly $1.3670, the high since September 2021 in late January. The momentum indicators are overextended, and although they have not turned lower, the month's advance is long in the tooth. A break of $1.3500 could signal scope for another cent decline.

Canada

Drivers: The rolling 30-day correlation between the changes of the US dollar-Canadian dollar exchange rate and the Dollar Index is more than twice as high as the correlation between the exchange rate and oil prices. The correlation with the Dollar Index is around 0.65, gradually rising from the late March low near 0.56. In Q1, the correlation reached nearly 0.85, the highest since May 2024. As counterintuitive as it may seem, since around the middle of last month, the US dollar has tended to appreciate against the Canadian dollar when oil prices are rising. The 30-day rolling correlation jumped to 0.47 last week, the highest since last August. There is also an element of risk. The rolling 30-day correlation is inverse with changes in the S&P 500. Around -0.36, it is the most in four months.

Data: Canada reports March CPI and February retail sales. Canada's headline CPI before the war was 1.8%, and the core stood at 2.0%. Prices are expected to have risen in March. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for the headline rate to jump to 2.6% and for the core rates to edge up. The March jobs report was uninspiring. The market recognizes that the central bank may sit on the sidelines for the next few months. Before the war began, the swaps market was discounting about a 40% chance of another cut (after cutting the target rate by 275 bp over the past two years). At the peak on March 20, the pricing in the swaps market was consistent with a little more than three hikes. Now it discounts one hike fully and almost 10% chance of a second.

Prices: The Canadian dollar has been on a run. It has risen in nine of the past ten sessions for almost a 2% gain. One way to appreciate the magnitude of the move, is to consider that the one-month implied volatility is about 4.5%, annualized. The US dollar reached CAD1.3650 before the weekend, a one-month low. It stalled and recovered to almost CAD1.3690. The greenback is coming down from around CAD1.3965 at the end of March, and the move seems advanced. The risk-reward may be shifting against chasing the Canadian dollar higher. The risks of a greenback bounce appear to be increasing.

Australia

Drivers: The broad movement in the US dollar seems to be the more important driver of the Australian dollar's exchange rate. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation of changes in the Aussie and the Dollar Index is around -0.75, near the most extreme since last September. It is positively correlated with the S&P 500, about 0.65, for the past 30 sessions. It has spent little time above 0.70 since last November. Changes in the Aussie and oil are around -0.40, the most since last September. Counterintuitively, the Aussie is inversely correlated with changes in Australia's two-year yield. It is near this year's low, near -0.22.

Data: The only data of note in the coming days is the preliminary PMI. Recall that in March, the manufacturing, services, and composite readings were all below the 50 boom/bust period. The disruption of the Middle East war likely had more impact than the two rate hikes, with the second taking place after the war began. The composite PMI plummeted to 46.6, the lowest since late 2023, from 52.5 in February. It is the first sub-50 reading since September 2024. Ironically, the manufacturing sector held up better than services. The manufacturing PMI fell for the second consecutive month, and March's 49.8 was the weakest since last October. The services PMI peaked in January at 56.3. It fell sharply in February to 52.8 and then fell nearly twice as much in March to 46.3, signaling the deepest contraction since November 2023. The central bank meets on May 5. The futures market has discounted almost 80% of another hike. We had leaned against the third consecutive rate hike, but the prospects of an end to the war in the Middle East and hawkish official comments forced a re-evaluation.

Prices: The Australian dollar reached its best level since mid-2022 at the end of last week. It poked above $0.7220 to culminate the three-week rally of a little more than 4.5%. It frayed the upper Bollinger Band (~$0.7215) but pulled back to settle around $0.7175. Initial support may be in the $0.7130 area, and a break could signal a corrective phase that could extend toward $0.7000.

Mexico

Drivers: The Mexican peso appears most sensitive to the risk environment. Changes in the dollar-peso exchange rate and the S&P 500 are around -0.80. This is the most extreme since 2020. Ironically, the changes in the USD against the peso and changes in the Australian dollar are almost -0.80. The correlation of the exchange rate and the Dollar Index is about 0.75, the most since last September. Rising oil prices are not helpful for the peso. The rolling 30-day correlation of changes in WTI and the dollar-peso exchange rate is a little below 0.50, a four-year high.

Data: Mexico's February retail sales are too dated to have much impact on the central bank's decision early next month. It is difficult to see it sustaining momentum after the 1% surge in January. The February IGAE economic activity report may not be particularly helpful either, given the disruption from the war. Mexico will also report March unemployment, which stood at 2.59% in February. It recorded last year's low at 2.22% in March, a record under the current time series that began in 1994. Remember, though, that over half of Mexican workers are in the informal economy.

Prices: The dollar initially was sold to a new low since the Middle East war began ahead of the weekend, reaching almost MXN17.1275. However, it reversed higher and closed above Thursday's high (~MXN17.2960). This is the kind of price action one looks for at the end of a sustained move. The MXN17.45-50 area may offer initial resistance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Buy 5 Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Dogs Of 40 March ReFa/Ro
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a REader FAvorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only ROgues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate are most valuable.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment.

The more tickers mentioned, the better these articles become.

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

In June a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team. YCharts, says: “At this time we do not have a filter or screener criterion to identify stocks that explicitly pay "qualified dividends" per IRS standards. However, I am happy to pass along your feedback to our team if this is something that can be added on. I can't guarantee any changes, but they use feedback like that to help determine what enhancements should be added in future updates.”

My ten (going on eleven) year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers and a celebratory anniversary book is planned.

Still, readers complain they can’t find my eleven Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

Summary and reference guides to 11 portfolios:

Here is an updated SA listing by date of all my indexes to portfolio postings in my Dogcatcher Investing Group:

In Investing Groups site:

October 3, 2016 for I

October 13, 2018 for II

October 7, 2018 for III

September 3, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 14, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

October 6, 2025 for XI (Elephant)

Incidentally, the Attention Portfolio (DOTW X), initiated October 11 2024, was filled October 10 2025 and will be fully funded by October 9, 2026.

The Elephant Folio (DOTW XI), initiated October 10, 2025 will be filled with 52 member stocks in October 9, 2026, and be fully funded by October 8, 2027.

Stay tuned for the first newly formatted Toy Dog Portfolio of tiny but mighty low priced dividend dogs.

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the Dogcatcher

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article: “Death by data…just get to the point already…

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, and all my other articles, less opaque.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently (4/17/26) priced at $25.50, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 4.37%! A little over $1k investment now buys 39.22 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 1.66 times the share price. And more good news, SIRI is out the doghouse this month because of positive returns for the year to date of 25.21%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 23.6 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my idea of share price equal to or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 3/27/26 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,184.10)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1,000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader, waxOn, suggested that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

This reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025 confirms the best use of my advice:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.”

The point of this ReFa/Ro article is: “Be More Dog!” Stay tuned, Toy Dog stock breeds have launched for you along with my Top Dogs of each month, quarter and year for the past ten years!

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.15% To 63.09% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By March, 2027.

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this March, 2026 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested and aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, for the ten highest-yielding stocks (as reported by YCharts) created the 2026-27 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to March, 2027 were:

MFA Financial (MFA) was projected to net $630.87, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% greater than the market as a whole.

Franklin BSP Realty Trust (FBRT) was projected to net $586.83, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% greater than the market as a whole.

Omnicom Group (OMC) was projected to net $351.14, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% less than the market as a whole.

Smurfit WestRock (SW) was projected to net $346.51, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for SW.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) was projected to net $329.06 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% greater than the market as a whole.

Kilroy Realty (KRC) was projected to net $266.46, based on annual dividends, the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% over the market as a whole.

Robert Half (RHI) was projected to net $265.02 based on the median of target prices estimated by 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

Ellington Financial (EFC) was projected to net $264.85, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 69% under the market as a whole.

VICI Properties (VICI) was projected to net $264.68, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% less than the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer LP (ET) was projected to net $241.47 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 35.47% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection become valued as ”dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs,” owing to their contrarian nature.

The March, 2026 ReFa/Ro By Total Returns

data.title: Sezzle: Positive Growth Momentum And An Improved Valuation
data.description:
This is a follow-up article for Sezzle (SEZL), which I last covered in June 2025. I warned investors in that last article that Sezzle's valuation was stretched and that the technicals looked bearish. The stock did decline by over 50% from when that article was published to November 2025.

Sezzle looks much better now from a valuation, growth, and technical perspective. The company is achieving strong growth through its strategies. All of this should help drive the stock higher.

Sezzle's Launch of Virtual Card in Canada

Sezzle's recent launch of its virtual card in Canada could be a positive catalyst for the stock. The virtual card allows users to have a buy now, pay later payment option. This is important because 74% of Sezzle users reported being more likely to shop in-store if Sezzle was available, according to a 2025 survey of Sezzle users.

This card can help to drive traffic and sales to brick-and-mortar retail stores. Then, consumers benefit by having a convenient payment option. So, it should be a win-win situation for consumers and businesses.

Consumers can easily add the Sezzle virtual card to their Apple Pay (AAPL) or Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) wallets. Businesses can easily benefit as well since over 90% of Canadian retailers already accept contactless payments.

Future Growth Outlook

Sezzle finished 2025 on a strong note as revenue increased 66% yoy to $450 million for the full year. Net income increased 69.5% yoy to $133 million. Sezzle achieved strong margin expansion for the year, which helped drive the strong gains in net income. The gross margin increased 62% in 2024 up to 73% in 2025. The operating margin increased from 51% to 59%, and the net income margin increased from 29% to 29.6% over the same time.

The margin gains were attributed to Sezzle's rigorous cost discipline. The company reduced non-transactional operating expenses from 30.4% in 2024 down to 26.3% in 2025. Of course, SEZL's strong revenue growth also had a positive impact on margins. Sezzle sees more opportunity to continue to scale going forward. This is a result of SEZL's core infrastructure acting as a force multiplier. Sezzle has the ability to grow revenue and margins faster than its overhead. Sezzle found a way to finance optimally to fund its growth in a cost-efficient manner through a revolving credit facility.

The company also has an ongoing antitrust suit that Sezzle can't discuss in detail since it is currently in progress. Sezzle did point out that they are seeing positive signs of the environment shifting. SEZL is pleased to see recent regulatory momentum. Sezzle did point out that they are in the discovery phase supported by external consultants and attorneys.

Sezzle's focus on subscription products is meant to drive long-term future growth. This is based on the company's analysis, which demonstrated that subscription users have higher lifetime values than on-demand users. SEZL pointed out that the subscribers made a commitment to the company when they chose to subscribe. Sezzle's subscriber growth increased 30% yoy in Q4 2025 and 18% sequentially. So, the company is achieving positive momentum.

Sezzle attributes disciplined targeted marketing as the strategy to achieve strong returns on investment. The company's current performance demonstrates a 6-month payback on its investments.

Sezzle's success with its returns is evident in its profitability metrics. Sezzle achieved an ROIC of 66% in 2025. The ROE was also strong at 103% last year, while the ROA was 33%. These all significantly outperformed the sector median ROIC of 7%, the ROE of 11%, and the ROA of 1.2%. Sezzle's strong returns can help drive earnings growth going forward. SEZL's returns quantify the effectiveness of the targeted marketing strategy.

Of course, Sezzle's efficiencies aren't only isolated to its targeted marketing. The company evaluates the entire organization for efficiencies and scaling opportunities. Another example of this is the use of AI to improve the customer experience. Sezzle believes that they won't be negatively impacted the way many SaaS companies have been. SEZL believes that the only way the company gets harmed by AI is if they don't enable it.

Sezzle is actually using as much AI as it can to increase efficiencies. SEZL stated that, as an example, they aim to use AI to turn their team of 400 into the equivalent of a team of 4,000 through efficiencies. SEZL is becoming a creator of AI by building its own proprietary engines instead of relying on outside vendors. Sezzle's use of AI enables the company to eliminate expensive 3rd-party costs while improving each aspect of its business. This includes the importance of improving consumer engagement to increase customer retention.

The company has evolved from just a Pay-in-4 product to an all-in-one consumer app. The consumer app now provides financial tools and shopping features designed to help consumers quickly find specific products at the optimal price with the best payment terms for their budget.

Sezzle is guiding for strong 2026 results. The company expects revenue to increase 25% to 30% in 2026. SEZL projects net income to rise about 28% to $170 million and EPS to increase 31% to $4.70. The guidance reflects Sezzle's confidence in continued momentum for its core business. It does not reflect any benefit from new products currently being developed. So, any new product launches could add to these estimates. This strong growth has the potential to catalyze the stock for strong gains if Sezzle meets/exceeds these estimates.

Valuation

At first glance, Sezzle appears to be trading at a premium. This is due to SEZL's forward PE of 15.8x, which is higher than the sector median of 10.5x. However, Sezzle's growth rate is higher than the forward PE. So, I feel that using the PEG ratio would be more appropriate. SEZL is trading with a one-year PEG of just 0.51 (forward PE of 15.8/2026 estimated EPS growth of 30.92%). A PEG below one indicates a bargain, as Sezzle's growth rate is much higher than the forward PE. For context, the sector median PEG is higher at 1.05.

The chart above illustrates Sezzle's stock price (purple line) vs. the EPS growth (orange line). SEZL's EPS has been on a strong uptrend. There was a point last year when the stock price got ahead of the EPS trendline. That reflected an overvaluation. However, the stock is now significantly trailing the EPS trendline. This shows an undervaluation, as the stock price needs to catch up to the EPS trend.

Technical Perspective

Sezzle's daily chart shows the strong rally that the stock experienced this week. All five days had gains. This brought the stock from the $50s up to the $80s within the week.

The RSI indicator is bullish as it moved past 50 and then 60. The RSI remains below the overbought level of 70. So, the stock probably has more room to move higher. Stocks with a lot of positive momentum can move deep into overbought territory.

The MACD is also in bullish mode as the blue line crossed above the red signal line while the histogram bars turned green. The increasing length of the histogram bars indicates rising momentum.

Sezzle's next earnings report for Q1 2026, scheduled for May 6, 2026, could be the next catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting Q1 revenue of $129 million and normalized EPS of $1.24. SEZL could meet/exceed these expectations considering the strong momentum that the company has been achieving on the top and bottom lines.

Risks for Sezzle

Sezzle bears all of the credit risk for its customers. Sezzle depends on customers spreading out payments over time. Credit losses are a significant portion of SEZL's operating expenses. Excessive credit failures from customers would have a negative impact on the company.

The buy-now, pay-later industry is subject to various regulations and scrutiny. Increased regulations for the industry could add to Sezzle's costs. More regulations could hinder Sezzle's ability to grow the business.

Competition remains an ongoing risk for Sezzle. The company competes with various payment apps and platforms. The industry has a low barrier to entry. So, competition could increase relatively easily with new entrants in the industry. Increased competition could take market share away from Sezzle and make it more difficult for the company to grow.

Sezzle's Investment Outlook

Sezzle's positive growth momentum has a good chance to continue for the foreseeable future. The company has a solid strategy with its subscription model. Sezzle's above-average expected revenue and earnings growth for 2026 has the potential to drive the stock for above-average gains. Sezzle's low valuation leaves more room to the upside for the stock. The technicals also support more upside momentum for the stock this year.

data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series offers a top-down view of the consumer staples sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: AEVEX: Drone Player Taking Off
data.description:
Shares of AEVEX (AVEX) have seen a spectacular move higher on the first day of trading, with investors liking the prospect of this drone producer. Technological and competitive developments are unfolding very rapidly here, making it difficult to see which company and technology has the upper hand, as AEVEX benefits from strong momentum here.

I like the momentum displayed by the business, as the company is seeing strong sales growth, higher margins, and trades at relatively compelling valuation multiples, even after a big jump on the first day of trading. This makes me upbeat as I see appeal, that mostly being relative appeal. I am willing to take a small speculative position, with an interest to learn more about the performance of the business.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Enabling Future Of UAS Dominance Strategy

AEVEX has a mission to partner with the US and its international government partners, designed to deliver advanced autonomous systems that ensure success on the battlefield while shaping the future of defense.

The company describes itself as a defense technology prime contractor to enable US Unmanned Aerial Systems (also known as UAS) strategy missions.

The company provides such solutions under two segments. The first are tactical systems, which are mostly used in tactical and actual battle situations, complemented by the global solutions business, used in specific missions. Tactical solutions are responsible for about three-quarters of sales, complemented by the smaller global solutions business.

The company serves a multitude of customers and platforms with over 10,000 systems delivered and committed through the end of this year, comprised of 6,200 deliveries made already and the remainder committed through the rest of this year. Its 100,000-square-foot production facilities have a production capacity of 1,000 units per month, ample space to rack up production here.

The company claims expertise under some key characteristics, including rapid innovation and development cycles, proprietary AI autonomy, aligned software and platforms, hardware integration, and both reliability and predictable production.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

AEVEX aimed to sell 16 million shares in a preliminary price range between $18 and $21 per share, with pricing seen just over the midpoint of the range at $20 per share. All the shares were sold by the company, which ended up fetching $320 million in gross proceeds; that is excluding the greenshoe option.

With some 112 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $2.24 billion equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net cash position of around $60 million, based on the pro forma balance sheet, for a $2.2 billion enterprise valuation. However, I see $335 million in mezzanine equity on the balance sheet as well, also defined as non-controlling interest on the balance sheet.

While the company sounds like an incredible growth story, it requires some explanation. Revenues rose some 10% in 2025 to $433 million, while gross profits were down 15% to $94 million, marking serious deleverage on that front. The latter is attributed to start-up costs for a new platform to be developed.

Operating income was reported at $8 million after a combined $19 million in amortization charges and contingent consideration, with an adjusted operating profit of $27 million resulting in thin margins. This compares to $63 million operating profit based on the same definition in 2024.

With shares having seen 35% gains on the first day of trading, at a current level of $27 per share, the enterprise valuation has risen to $3.0 billion, or about $3.3 billion if mezzanine equity is included. That results in a roughly 7.5 times sales multiple, yet these are low-margin activities here. However, the 2025 results tell only part of the story, with the funded backlog essentially tripling between the end of 2024 and 2025, reported at just over half a billion.

Moreover, the quarterly revenue cadence in 2025 has been promising, with revenues reported at $53 million, $101 million, $121 million, and $157 million, respectively, during the year. Based on the fourth quarter, revenues trend around $600 million each year, with realistic operating profits trending around $100 million, as that yields a 5-6 times multiple and about 30-33 times operating profit multiple.

Very interesting are the preliminary first-quarter results, which indicate revenues between $200-$208 million, for a run rate around $800 million, or a 4x sales multiple. Adjusted EBITDA is seen around $35 million, for margins in the high-teens, all looking quite encouraging.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks in the offering are plentiful. The biggest ones (in my view) include customer concentration (with the US government making up 80% of sales), reliance on military spending, technology development, stiff competition, complexity, potential for errors, and others. Notably, competition and technological changes are a real risk.

Competition includes many established defense names, but also pure-play companies like AeroVironment (AVAV) as frankly a great deal of cost-effective innovation seems to be developed in active war zones, notably Ukraine. A larger peer like AeroVironment, which I covered recently, is valued around $10 billion, valued at around a 6 times sales multiple. This peer posts EBITDA margins around 10%. In that light, AEVEX trades at a big relative discount in terms of sales multiples, EBITDA multiples, while its growth rates are superior.

This makes me upbeat, certainly from a relative perspective. The spectacular sales growth, with revenues quadrupling and trading around 4 times sales, makes me relatively upbeat. Despite the huge opening day returns, a small speculative position seems warranted. To build conviction in the investment thesis, I would need to be more comfortable with the sustainable pace of growth and operational leverage to be demonstrated upon, yet a small position acting as a watch function seems fine here.

data.title: Beazer Homes USA: Downgrading Is The Right Choice Due To Leverage And Market Concerns
data.description:
One of my favorite things about value investing is the fact that, even when fundamentals are worsening, you can experience upside if a stock was purchased at a low enough price. A great example of this can be seen by looking at Beazer Homes USA (BZH). Back in November of last year, I told investors that the plunge in share price that the company experienced offered an opportunity for upside. I ended up rating it a ‘buy’ in that article. Since then, the stock has risen 9%, meaningfully outperforming the 3.1% increase that the S&P 500 saw. This is in spite of the fact that fundamentals have continued to worsen for the business. In the near term, we could see further deterioration caused by difficult market conditions. And I do think investors would be wise to take that seriously. I wouldn't go so far as to say that this deterioration deserves pessimism when it comes to this particular name. But I do think that, after experiencing the bump up in share price, downgrading it to a ‘hold’ would be prudent, especially in light of the overall balance sheet situation of the business.

Of course, it is always possible that I am being premature and downgrading it. However, I am not the only one who seems to be cautious. Later this month, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The expectation that analysts have is that revenue and profitability will worsen. If something big comes out of the woodwork, something that justifies more optimism, I will happily upgrade the stock once again. But for now, I think that taking the more cautious approach with my ‘hold’ rating is justified by the data.

No Longer a Great Home for Your Money

The newest data that investors have regarding Beazer Homes USA covers through the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. In fact, this is the only new data that we have available. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $363.5 million. This unfortunately represents a decline of 22.5% compared to the $469 million that the business reported a year earlier. This was in spite of the fact that the average price of a home sold by the business rose from $507,600 to $513,900. And it was driven, according to management, by a plunge in the number of homes closed from 907 to 700. As revenue dropped, profitability declined. The chart above shows that net profits and cash flows worsened across the board for the company.

This is not the only data point to worsen for the business. As the number of net new orders dropped from 932 to 763, the number of units in backlog for the company declined from 1,507 to 1,008. The only thing helping to offset this from a forward-looking perspective is the fact that the average price of a home in backlog increased from $541,500 to $568,700. Management has been very deliberate about how they are approaching these challenging market conditions. The firm is pushing, for instance, to boost its margins by 300 basis points throughout the year. A continued differentiation is being made by management as they continue to focus on their fully solar communities that they claim already have strong demand and higher margins. This actually makes a lot of sense when you consider that the appeal of such homes would be utility savings over the long haul. But at a time when consumers are under pressure, you would expect higher prices to push demand lower. And sure enough, that is what we have seen.

The good news is that Beazer Homes USA is not in this boat alone. The home building industry in general is under pressure right now. Years of inflationary pressures and high interest rates, not to mention now a weakening economy and the impact of tariffs, have caused home prices to fall as demand drops. Housing completions, for instance, ended up dropping 7.5% in January of this year compared to the same time last year. It is true that housing starts were 9.5% above what they were the year before. But it is my assertion that this is the result of home builders trying to front-load construction activity in order to boost cash flows in the near term as they try and weather the storm and reduce the risk of rising cancellation rates.

More telling, unfortunately, is the fact that building permits ended up dropping 5.8% year over year in January. And this is in spite of the fact that interest rates have declined over the last year, which should in theory push demand up. We are starting to see cancellation rates rise for the company also. From the first quarter of 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, they popped from 16.5% to 18.3%. This follows a drop from 17.7% in 2024 to 17.3% in 2025.

On April 30th, management will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of the year. They expect around 1,100 new home orders and 800 closings. For context, in 2025 for the second quarter, the number of homes delivered came in at 1,089, and the number of homes ordered totaled 1,098. So even though the company is guiding toward a similar number of home orders, closings will be weaker year over year.

Speaking of the second quarter, in the table above, you can see certain results that the company achieved for the 2025 fiscal year. During the second quarter, revenue was $565.3 million. But because of the expectation that closings will decline, analysts believe that sales will come in at only $448.4 million this year. This would mean a drop in earnings per share from $0.42 to negative $0.72, taking the company from a net profit of $12.8 million to a loss of $20.8 million. The table also shows certain cash flow metrics for the second quarter of 2025. If revenue and profitability both do drop this year, then cash flows will almost certainly show signs of deteriorating also.

Because we don't have a firm grasp of what the rest of the year will look like, the best thing that we can do is to value the company in relation to results from 2025. In the chart above, you can see how the firm is priced relative to earnings and cash flows and how other similar companies compare to it. The 2025 approach makes the company look to be somewhere between slightly undervalued and fairly valued. But considering that 2026 will almost certainly be worse than 2025 was, the company will be pushed into the fair value category at this rate.

Relative to other similar firms, the stock seems to be mixed. It's actually cheaper than the other five businesses relative to operating cash flow. But it's around the middle of the group on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, while it is the second most expensive when it comes to the price-to-earnings approach. I did a side, in the chart above, to look at the picture through the lens of the price-to-book multiple. In this respect, the company actually looks dirt cheap, trading at just half of book value. But there is some justification behind this. In the chart below, we can see that it has a net leverage ratio of 6.35. That is well above any of the other five institutions that I compared it to, and it means that the market is discounting the stock in relation to net assets specifically because of the leverage situation that the business is experiencing.

Takeaway

In the past, I was bullish about Beazer Homes USA. But I do think that, in light of changes that we have seen since I last wrote about the company, a more cautious approach is justified now. Based on the data provided, I am becoming a bit concerned, especially considering the amount of leverage the business has. And when you consider that we are nowhere near the bottom in this market, I think it would be prudent to downgrade it to a ‘hold’ at this time.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: The 7,000 Era Begins Amid Triple Record Highs
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 (SPX) officially entered the 7,000 era this week, capping off a historic 4.5% surge that represents its best weekly performance since May 2025. By closing green every day this week and notching three new record highs, the index has now secured its third consecutive weekly gain, the longest winning streak seen since last October. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new, then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since April 8th, 2026, and above the 200-day moving average since April 8th, 2026. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st, 2025.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, 2025, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.18%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index that includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 4.1% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 6.1% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

data.title: Apple Among 15 Companies To Announce Dividend Increases In The Second Half Of April
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of March, I provided predictions for 9 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in the first half of April. In this article I’ll look at another 15 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in the second half of April. Before I get to my predictions for the next two weeks, let's see how I did with my predictions (you can see the article with my original predictions here.)

(All yields are based on stock prices at the market close on Friday, April 17th.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements in the First Half of April

AON plc (AON) – 15 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 10.1 – 12.8% increase to $3.28 - $3.36

Actual: 10.1% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 0.99%

The Irish insurance broker continues to compound its dividend at 10% annually.

American Water Works Co., Inc. (AWK) – 17 years

Prediction: 6.9 – 9.1% increase to $3.54 - $3.61

Actual: Deferred to the 2nd half of April

The New Jersey-based utility should announce its next annual increase when it reports earnings on April 30th.

Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) – 22 years

Prediction: 11.5 – 13.1% increase to $5.80 - $5.88

Actual: 13.1% increase to $5.8

Forward yield: 0.59%

As expected, the wholesale retailer continues to grow its payout at 12-13% annually.

H. B. Fuller Company (FUL) – 56 years

Prediction: 5.3 – 7.4% increase to $0.99 - $1.01

Actual: 4.3% increase to $0.98

Forward yield: 1.52%

Dividend growth continues to slow at the specialty chemical company as this year’s payout increase is down from 5.6% last year.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) – 64 years

Prediction: 5.8 – 6.9% increase to $5.50 - $5.56

Actual: 3.1% increase to $5.36

Forward yield: 2.29%

While disappointing, the increase from Johnson & Johnson extends its dividend growth streak to 64 years.

Procter & Gamble Co. (PG) – 70 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 3.0 – 5.0% increase to $4.354 - $4.4386

Actual: 3.0% increase to $4.354

Forward yield: 2.96%

The consumer products company hits a milestone of 70 years of dividend growth with this boost.

Southern Company (SO) – 24 years

Prediction: 2.7 – 3.4% increase to $3.04 - $3.06

Actual: Deferred to the 2nd half of April

Atlanta-based Southern Company should announce its next annual dividend increase in the second half of the month.

Sonoco Products Co. (SON) – 43 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 3.8% increase to $2.16 - $2.20

Actual: 1.9% increase to $2.16

Forward yield: 3.76%

This is the third straight year of 4-cent annual dividend growth at the packaging and container manufacturer.

Travelers Companies Inc. (TRV) – 24 years

Prediction: 5.0 – 6.3% increase to $4.62 - $4.68

Actual: 13.6% increase to $5.00

Forward yield: 1.66%

I expected the insurer to focus on share buybacks. And they did, buying back $2 billion of shares during the most recent quarter. But the board also decided to approve the largest dividend boost since 2012.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in the Second Half of April

There are 15 long-term dividend growth companies that I expect to announce annual increases in the first half of April. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Apple Inc. (AAPL) – 13 years of dividend growth

The massive consumer electronics company is one of the best-known companies on the planet, with worldwide sales of nearly $450 billion annually. Apple breaks its business segments out geographically into the Americas; Europe, which includes India, the Middle East and Africa; Greater China, including Hong Kong and Taiwan; Japan; and the Rest of the Asia Pacific. The company saw sales grow across all geographic segments, with worldwide sales up more than 6% in 2025. Apple did even better in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, which included the Christmas season – sales were up more than 15% as iPhone sales led the way.

With this rate of sales growth, earnings have followed. Apple’s dedication to buying back shares has also boosted EPS: EPS in 2025 were up 23% to $7.49 and grew another 18% in the first quarter of 2026. So with this level of earnings growth, can investors expect a return to double-digit percentage dividend growth? I think that’s unlikely for two primary reasons. First, as I noted above, Apple has a consistent and aggressive share buyback program. Since 2021, the company has reduced the number of outstanding shares by 10% and since 2017 the share count has dropped by more than 25%. The second reason is that Apple is investing a great deal of free cash flow in R&D, specifically in artificial intelligence research. Apple reported R&D expenses grew 10% in 2025 and by more than 30% in the first quarter of 2026. Apple has established a steady, if low, dividend growth rate of 5% over the last half-decade. With the company using cash flow for share repurchases and R&D, I expect another year of dividend growth in the mid-single digit percentages.

Prediction: 4.8 – 7.7% increase to $1.09 - $1.12

Predicted Forward Yield: 0.40 – 0.41%

And here are my predictions for 14 other long-term dividend growth companies which should announce annual increases in the second half of April:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
Ameriprise Financial Inc. (AMP) 20 Financial - Asset Management 8.8% - 10.0% $6.96 - $7.04
The investment manager continues to grow at double-digits. Ameriprise just announced that it has $1.4 trillion in assets under management, which drove adjusted EPS growth of 14% in 2025. Along with compounding its dividend at 9% annually, the company aggressively repurchases its stock – it’s bought back more than 17% of outstanding shares since 2021. Although last year’s 8% increase was a little lower than normal, I expect this year’s dividend boost to return to the normal 9 – 10% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.53 - 1.54%
Avery Dennison Corporation (AVY) 15 Consumer Cyclical - Packaging & Containers 4.3% - 6.4% $3.92 - $4.00
Avery Dennison is a specialized materials company whose business includes self-adhesive labelling and radio-frequency IDs. The company has compounded its dividend by roughly 10% annually over the last decade. But at 7%, last year’s payout boost was below the long-term average. This year, I think we can expect dividend growth to fall further – the company’s sales growth and EPS growth came in at only 1%. Avery Dennison also focuses on share buybacks and has bought back more than 7% of shares in the last 5 years. For these reasons, I expect investors to see a boost in the mid-single digit percentages as dividend growth continues to slow. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.27 - 2.32%
Factset Research Systems Inc. (FDS) 27 Financial - Financial Data & Stock Markets 4.5% - 6.4% $4.60 - $4.68
The financial intelligence and data company continues to consistently grow revenues and earnings, with 2025 revenues up 5% and adjusted EPS up 3%. The company’s looking at a 6% growth in both revenues and adjusted EPS for 2026. With a payout ratio around 25% and a small share buyback program (FactSet has repurchased less than 2% of outstanding shares since 2021), the company is well-positioned for another dividend increase in the mid-single digit percentages, similar to last year’s 6% increase. Predicted Forward Yield:1.98 - 2.01%
First Financial Bankshares, Inc. (FFIN) 15 Financial - Regional Banks 5.3% - 10.5% $0.80 - $0.84
This regional bank saw good EPS growth in 2025 due to a big drop in charge-offs from bad accounts. This more than offset flat account and loan growth. EPS grew more than 13% year-over-year in 2025 to $1.77. With the company’s current dividend of 76 cents, this gives First Financial a healthy payout ratio of 43% and leaves room for another year of dividend growth. I expect the company to continue to grow its annual dividend by 4 to 6 cents. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.46 - 2.59%
W. W. Grainger Inc. (GWW) 54 Industrials - Industrial Distribution 9.7% - 11.1% $9.92 - $10.04
The industrial distributor took a loss in the third quarter when it exited its U.K. business, resulting in EPS falling 8.6% despite 5% sales growth. Ignoring the one-time costs, EPS grew about 1% to $39.48. While the small adjusted EPS growth might be a cause for concern, Grainger is expecting a minimum of 20% EPS growth in 2026. The company is also expecting to repurchase $1 billion in shares, building on the 7% of share repurchases completed over the last 5 years. With the expectations of outstanding EPS growth next year, I anticipate a good 10% increase from the company. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.85 - 0.86%
International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) 30 Technology - Information Technology Services 3.0% - 4.2% $6.92 - $7.00
The technology company has suffered from inconsistent earnings per share, hitting a low of $1.80 in 2022, bouncing back to $8.14 in 2023, and then falling to $6.43 in 2024. With earnings jumping all over the place, IBM chose to hold its dividend growth to 4 cents a year for the last half decade, a rate below 1% a year. Earnings rebounded 74% in 2025 to $11.14 and adjusted EPS grew 12% to $11.59, on the double-digit revenue growth in the Software and Infrastructure segments. But while IBM’s financials are improving, the last several years have taken its toll. I expect the company will accelerate the dividend growth, but only to the 3 – 4% range while the company waits to see if the rebound sticks. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.73 - 2.76%
Lithia Motors, Inc. (LAD) 15 Consumer Cyclical - Auto & Truck Dealerships 7.3% - 9.1% $2.36 - $2.40
The auto and truck retailer continues to grow through acquisitions, including recent purchases of dealerships in Tennessee and Oregon. Prior to last year, Lithia had a history of 10%+ increases; the company broke that streak with a 4% increase as EPS fell for the second year in a row. The company reversed the fall in EPS in 2025 with 10% growth to more than $32 a share. While this might drive the company to return to 10%+ increases, Lithia continues to use its free cash flow to aggressively expand through acquisitions and as well as buying back shares. I expect investors to see an acceleration in dividend growth, but it'll still be below 10%. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.83 - 0.85%
Metlife Inc. (MET) 13 Financial - Life Insurance 4.4% - 5.7% $2.37 - $2.40
While Metlife is primarily an insurance company, the company also manages retirement and pension plans for other businesses. The bulk of the company’s earnings come from two business segments: (1) Group Benefits and Retirement and (2) Income Solutions, the former of which grew net earnings by 5%. Overall, Metlife grew its adjusted EPS by nearly 10% in 2025. However, the company has a consistent dividend growth rate in the 4 – 5% range and uses the rest of its free cash flow to buy back shares. And the company is buying back shares aggressively, to the tune of 20% of outstanding shares since 2021 and 37% since 2017. Given this history, I expect Metlife to boost its payout by about 5% this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 3.04 - 3.07%
MSA Safety Inc. (MSA) 54 Industrials - Security & Protection Services 3.8% - 5.7% $2.20 - $2.24
The manufacturer and marketer of safety equipment posted a loss of 10% in EPS on sales growth of 4% in 2025. Nearly all of the sales growth came from the international market and EPS growth there partially offset a loss in the company’s Americas segment. But business was good enough for MSA Safety to announce a $500 million share buyback program in February. The company also is actively spending some of its free cash flow on strategic acquisitions, so I think that dividend growth will be muted this year, with MSA announcing a mid-single digit percentage increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.27 - 1.29%
Parker-Hannifin Corporation (PH) 69 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 10.0% - 11.1% $7.92 - $8.00
The industrial engineering company is on the brink of becoming one of only a handful of companies that has grown its dividend for 70 years. Along with a long history of dividend growth, Parker-Hannifin has an excellent compounded growth rate of 11% over the last decade. Investors are likely to see another good payout boost this year – the company grew adjusted EPS by 7% in 2025 and is guiding to 11% growth in 2026 on 6% sales growth. I expect another double-digit percentage increase from Parker-Hannifin. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.80 - 0.81%
Pool Corporation (POOL) 15 Industrials - Industrial Distribution 1.6% - 3.2% $5.08 - $5.16
The aptly named pool supply company has established a record of quickly growing its payout but that’s changed over the last couple of years. After peaking in 2022, EPS have fallen every year since then, hitting $10.85 last year on flat sales. While the company is expecting a bit of a rebound this year, the peak earnings growth guidance of 3% isn’t enough to reverse the slowdown in dividend growth. I expect dividend growth to slow from last year’s 4% increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.23 - 2.27%
Portland General Electric Company (POR) 19 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.9% - 4.8% $2.16 - $2.20
The data center growth in Portland GE’s area of operations has driven good revenue growth. To meet growing electricity demand, the company recently acquired generation operations from PacifiCorp. Unfortunately, the company is issuing additional shares to fund the acquisition, adding to the share count that’s up 40% since 2023. While the company continues to affirm 5 – 7% long-term EPS growth, adjusted EPS were down 3% in 2025. With adjusted EPS guidance of up to $3.53 for 2026, the company might just about hit the lower end of the 5 – 7% growth guidance, but until the 2026 numbers come in I expect dividend growth to slow from last year’s 5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.12 - 4.20%
RTX Corporation (RTX) 32 Industrials - Aerospace & Defense 5.9% - 7.4% $2.88 - $2.92
RTX is benefiting from increased military spending, with sales and profits up 18% in the company’s Raytheon segment and in the military portion of the Pratt & Whitney segment. The company just reported full company adjusted EPS up 10% in 2025 and guided to 6 – 7% adjusted EPS growth for 2026. This level of growth is consistent with RTX’s 5-year compounded dividend growth rate of 7.5%. Last year, RTX announced an 8% dividend boost; I think this year, the company will announce a slightly smaller increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.47 - 1.49%
Sysco Corporation (SYY) 56 Consumer Defensive - Food Distribution 2.8% - 4.6% $2.22 - $2.26
The global food distribution company continues to grow sales at a 3 – 4% clip annually, with adjusted EPS growing at the same rate. Sysco reported adjusted EPS of $4.46 in 2025, up 3.5% year-over-year. The company’s also guiding to adjusted EPS growth of 3% in 2026. In addition to paying dividends, Sysco spends its free cash flow on repurchasing shares, buying back more than 6% of its shares since 2021. While Sysco has a 6% compounded dividend growth rate over the last decade, over the last 5 years that growth rate drops to less than 4%. With the drop in earnings now, I expect this year’s boost will be closer to the 5-year growth rate. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.91 - 2.96%

Summary

Despite the relatively low number of companies announcing annual dividend increases in the first two weeks of April, three of them were in the double digit percentages. Insurer Travelers surprised to the upside with a 14% boost, while insurance broker AON and warehouse retailer Costco continued their historical patterns of double-digit growth. And even among the smaller dividend increases, there were two notable events: Dividend Kings Johnson & Johnson and Procter & Gamble extended their dividend growth streaks to 64 and 70 years, respectively.

Looking forward to the latter half of April, I expect most of the increases to be relatively small, in the low to mid-single digit percentages. Perhaps the biggest event will be the anticipated announcement from Apple. Historically the company’s annual dividend increase has bounced between late April and early May, and I expect this year’s increase to be in late April and in the mid-single digits. Other notable increases that I’m expecting include announcements from Dividend Kings Parker-Hannifin, Sysco, MSA Safety, and W. W. Grainger, with Grainger’s and Parker-Hannifin’s boosts to be at or above 10%.
